text:
I have been relatively quiet this week in the midst of such volatility and with very good reason because I am on a break in China (my very maiden trip here).

What I have seen and experienced so far related to energy:

1️⃣ High speed train reaching a speed of 348 km/h from Beijing to Tianjin, electric powered, no less

2️⃣ Busy roads and highways, quieter than most because of influx of EV vehicles. But it doesn't mean no traditional transport fuelled cars though.

3️⃣ Lots of English words everywhere but not much spoken (good luck with ordering food in a random shop!)

4️⃣ Amazing skyscrapers and construction in many places. Tianjin in particular surprised me with vibrant lights and laser show on the facade of multiple buildings 

5️⃣ A strong sense of culture steeped in thousands of years of history yet striving for innovation and hunger for success

Can't say I have witnessed demand destruction first hand.

Photo taken at Temple of Heaven where I heard some Americans talking to a local about Elon Musk being there recently. I was thinking of Trump more as I walked up those steps!

#oott

MAP OF THE DAY: Iran has published a new chart showing two shipping lanes in Hormuz to the south of the ones that Tehran unveiled weeks ago. The new lanes are to the north of the ones used before the war. The Omani lane remains in operation too. 

Before (left) and now (right) https://t.co/oDIVgmolgN

Brent bears build near-record short position on peace memo, shifting balance of risks

Investors have become very bearish about the outlook for crude oil prices following the conclusion of a memorandum of understanding between the United States and Iran to terminate their war and re-open the Strait of Hormuz.

Hedge funds and other money managers sold the equivalent of 95 million barrels in ICE Brent futures and options over the seven days ending on June 16 (a period that included announcement of the memorandum).

Fund managers sold Brent at the fastest rate for more than a year, and at one of the fastest weekly rates since the coronavirus pandemic ...

This is a short extract from my newsletter emailed to subscribers earlier today. If you would like to read my newsletters in full, three times a week, you can subscribe via my website https://t.co/YjJTctVocX

Drinking from the cup of poison: White House reluctantly catches up with the oil market

Oil traders and hedge funds increasingly anticipated the imminent re-opening of the Strait of Hormuz for more than a month before the United States and Iran concluded a memorandum of understanding ending their war on June 15.

With petroleum inventories at home running down rapidly, top U.S. policymakers faced an increasingly urgent deadline to decide whether to reach an agreement with Iran or attempt to re-open the Strait fully by force. 

Ultimately, the White House seems to have decided the embarrassment of a diplomatic deal achieving less than its stated war aims was a lesser evil than the potential risks of trying to force the Strait open against hostile fire. 

In recent weeks, the threats, blockades, clandestine convoys and attacks on missile and drone facilities near the waterway can all be seen as an effort to escape making this unpleasant choice. 

But after exploring various options, the White House appears to have concluded none of the military ones was acceptable and it would have to settle for a deal achieving much less than it initially hoped when the war started.  

In reaching that decision, the White House was catching up with a conclusion many oil traders and fund managers had reached weeks earlier ...

The cost of shipping #containers continues to surge, with the Drewry Composite Index rising 12% over the past week, extending its seven-week gain to nearly 80%. Freight rates on the key Shanghai–New York and Shanghai–Rotterdam routes have almost doubled during that period, driven by a "perfect storm" of overlapping geopolitical, regulatory, and seasonal supply chain pressures.

In essence, a surge that isn't driven by a boom in global consumer spending, but rather an artificial rush of buying for vessel space to outrun tariffs, regulatory deadlines, and extended transit times at a time of elevated fuel costs. 

The index has risen 79% YTD and 21% over the past twelve months.

Maritime traffic via the Strait of Hormuz will only return to normal levels when mines are removed and traditional routes restored, some of the world's largest shipping organizations said June 18 while calling for more clarity on transit rules and coordination among authorities to allow safe passage. https://t.co/xouak9yKnr
https://t.co/XEMh6w66cm

3/6 🛢️ THE OIL DOWNTREND: The Iran-US peace memorandum is formally signed, removing the Hormuz uncertainty. WTI crude fell toward $74.41, solidifying the clearest, most high-conviction downtrend in any asset class today. Goldman Sachs just cut its Q4 Brent forecast from $90 to $80.

Brent crude is set for an 8% to 9% weekly decline after Israel and Hezbollah agreed to a ceasefire that took effect Friday afternoon, easing immediate fears of supply disruption in the Middle East. The benchmark traded around 79 to 80 dollars a barrel, down more than 1% on the day, as tanker traffic resumed through the Strait of Hormuz following a U.S.-Iran interim deal signed earlier in the week. West Texas Intermediate followed suit, with the front-month contract heading for a near 10% drop as traders priced in a faster return of Iranian barrels and reduced risk premium from the region in Israel🇮🇱 and Lebanon🇱🇧.

The selloff marks the steepest weekly fall since early April, reversing gains driven by the conflict that began in March. Analysts say the market is now focused on whether the ceasefire holds and how quickly shipping through Hormuz returns to pre-war levels, which carry about 20% of global oil and LNG. A memorandum between Washington and Tehran sets a 60-day window to negotiate a lasting truce and restore full transit capacity within 30 days, though U.S. officials canceled Friday’s planned talks in Switzerland, adding uncertainty. Goldman Sachs expects Gulf exports to normalize by end-July, but traders caution that any flare-up could quickly put a floor under prices again.

- World Business News.
Imagine the scenes if we see $100+ Brent Crude again this year

Because of Juneteenth, major U.S. cash equity markets (NYSE and Nasdaq) were completely closed, and the CME/CBOT did not settle new prices today. Consequently, this week's book effectively closed out with Thursday afternoon's settlement numbers as markets head into the long weekend.

Market Summary

Equities
Wall Street was completely dark today in observance of the Juneteenth national holiday. Cash equities finished the active portion of the week mixed, with technology stocks holding steady while broader industrial sectors experienced a minor pre-holiday pull-back. Trading volumes will remain completely frozen until Monday morning's opening bell.

Energy & Macros
WTI Crude Oil markets went into the holiday block holding near the 85.50 per barrel mark. Because of the federal holiday, standard government inventory data drops were pushed back, keeping immediate energy trade quiet. Biofuel plants and regional truck lines are wrapping up the week with a close eye on retail diesel spreads, which have shown mild contraction ahead of the mid-summer shipping lull.

Metals
Precious metals saw an early electronic halt today, freezing weekly action. Gold managed to preserve its baseline above 4,200 per ounce heading into the long weekend, while spot silver finished the active trading cycle maintaining its floor near 28.20 per ounce.

Grains & Livestock

Corn: Board prices remained unchanged today due to the holiday closure. December new-crop contracts are entering the weekend still heavy, stuck below key moving averages as favorable mid-June weather across the broader Corn Belt limits any immediate speculative short-covering.

Soybeans: The oilseed complex saw zero trade today. The market is heading into next week balancing a quiet export window against a highly active domestic crush pace, keeping local physical processors busier than the flat board suggests.

Wheat: Grains were entirely paused today. The wheat complex remains highly reactive to early harvest progress reports coming out of the southern plains, with traders waiting to gauge yield realities versus initial crop damage models.

Livestock: CME livestock pits were closed today with no new settlements derived. August Live Cattle went into the holiday holding at 240.95 cents per pound, while August Feeders remain pinned at 357.05 cents per pound, leaving physical auction premiums intact over the weekend.

Inside Look

With Chicago taking a breather for the holiday, the real story today is the silence on the board versus the noise in the fields. Excellent regional weather has given the electronic charts a reason to drift, but the physical basis landscape across central Nebraska remains tight as a drum. Elevators in the O'Neill region aren't budging on old-crop cash bids because they see the local moisture levels failing to match the idealized national weather maps. In the cattle barns, local buyers are heading into the long weekend facing firm country pricing on fat cattle, proving once again that processing plants are short on immediate numbers regardless of what the fund managers are doing in Illinois.

#AgMarketing #Commodities #LivestockMarket

Something about canoe tipping

We're at the most short Brent crude the market has ever been https://t.co/LhJYd9VfmU

Weekly Futures Surprises for the week of June 15–19, 2026. This report tracks performance from the Monday open through the abbreviated Friday close due to the Juneteenth federal holiday, which brought a 12:00 PM CT early trading halt to equity and energy pits.

Top Futures Instrument: June Nasdaq 100 (/NQ)
Technology futures staged a spectacular mid-week breakout to lead global markets, driven by a massive semiconductor rally. Intel’s surprise announcement that it will manufacture chips for Apple domestically triggered a wave of buying across the entire sector, easily overriding broader macro concerns during a light holiday session.

Monday Open: 30,124.75
Friday Close: 30,768.50
Weekly Change: +643.75 (+2.14%)
Bias: Strongly Bullish
Trend: Record Extension

Outlook: The dynamic support level has confidently moved up to 30,400. Broad structural demand for artificial intelligence hardware and localized manufacturing continues to keep buyers firmly in control.

Biggest Surprise Loser: June WTI Crude Oil (/CL)
Crude oil suffered a massive, historic drubbing this week, sliding roughly 7.7% to post its steepest weekly decline of the year. The surprise was the swift signing of an interim agreement between the U.S. and Iran to fully reopen the Strait of Hormuz, which immediately caught long-side speculators off guard and triggered a rush to unwind supply-disruption hedges.

Monday Open: $84.85
Friday Close: $76.85
Weekly Change: -$8.00 (-9.43%)
Bias: Strongly Bearish
Trend: Structural Breakdown

Outlook: The long-standing war premium has completely evaporated from energy markets. Technical models show oil is heavily exposed to a further drift toward $72.00 as stranded commercial tankers resume transit through the shipping channel.

The Top Markets Surprise: The Strait of Hormuz Peace Accord

The single most impactful event across global finance this week was the sudden breakthrough agreement to end the maritime blockade in the Middle East Gulf. The deal instantly clears a major backlog of shipping vessels and lifts restrictions on regional crude supply, releasing millions of barrels back into the global market and fundamentally resetting volatility indexes across multiple asset classes.

The Juneteenth Holiday Liquidity Drain
With U.S. banking institutions and cash equity desks entirely closed on Friday, trading volumes thinned out drastically ahead of the midday futures halt. While this lack of structural liquidity amplified early morning price swings, it left benchmark equity and interest rate products pinned near their late-Thursday settlement bands.

Bitcoin's Range-Bound Stability
Bitcoin futures (/BTC) spent the week in a quiet consolidation pattern, settling just below the $63,000 handle. While tech equities pushed to aggressive highs and oil collapsed, digital assets completely avoided the broader market noise, remaining well-supported above their $60,000 baseline despite minor holiday volume.

Weekly Scorecard:

Nasdaq 100 (/NQ): +2.14% (Semiconductor-Led Breakout)
S&P 500 (/ES): +1.10% (Holiday Trading Rise)
Gold (/GC): -0.45% (Defensive Unwinding)
Bitcoin (/BTC): +0.15% (Holiday Range Bind)
Crude Oil (/CL): -9.43% (Hormuz Reopening Collapse)

#FuturesMarket #HormuzPeace #MarketUpdate

Friday Market Closing Report: Friday, June 19, 2026

Wall Street wrapped up a truncated trading week with solid gains across all major benchmarks, as US cash equity and bond markets remained completely closed today in observance of the Juneteenth federal holiday. Final weekly settlement prices for domestic stocks were locked in at Thursday's closing bell, securing a strong weekly rebound powered by massive tech gains and sweeping geopolitical relief. On the week, the tech-heavy Nasdaq led the charge, while the S&P 500 crossed back above a major psychological threshold to log its best multi-day stretch of the month.

Top Markets Story: Broad Tech Recovery Offsets Geopolitical Headwinds

Technology companies put the broader market on their back this week, successfully erasing almost all of the steep valuation losses suffered during the early June flush. Investor sentiment received an enormous structural boost after Intel (INTC) surged more than 10% on news of a major domestic production partnership to manufacture advanced AI microchips. The return of aggressive sector buying spread quickly to industry bellwethers, allowing mega-caps to fully digest hawkish monetary commentary from the Federal Reserve and safely insulate index valuations ahead of the long holiday weekend.

Futures and Commodities

While US floor trading was dark on Friday, electronic commodity and energy markets remained open for a truncated holiday session. The trading pits experienced sharp afternoon reversals as global macro funds reacted to late-breaking diplomatic developments coming out of Western Europe.

Top Futures Story: US-Iran Memorandum Inks Ceasefire and Reopens Hormuz Chokehold

The global energy landscape shifted dramatically following the formal signing of a landmark bilateral memorandum between Washington and Tehran. The historic agreement establishes a strict 60-day window to draft a permanent peace treaty and guarantees the immediate, unhindered reopening of the Strait of Hormuz to international oil tanker traffic. While news of a temporary delay in supplementary Swiss nuclear talks initially pushed crude prices up early Friday, a secondary ceasefire agreement between Israel and Hezbollah cooled safe-haven bidding. International benchmark Brent crude slid off its weekly highs to settle near $81.00 a barrel, while domestic West Texas Intermediate (WTI) finished just under $78.00, completely unwinding the dense shipping risk premium.

Market Mover Summary

Tech Resurgence: Intel (INTC) spearheaded the semiconductor sector's weekly breakout, providing a powerful tailwind for the entire hardware supply chain.
SpaceX Stabilization: Following its historic $2 trillion public market debut, SpaceX (SpaceX) faced moderate institutional profit-taking, sliding over 3% as early momentum traders normalized their positions.
Retail Underbelly: National logistics firms and heavy industrial lines caught a strong end-of-week bid as the sharp plunge in global fuel costs promised significant relief for corporate shipping margins.
Precious Metals Flush: Gold futures plunged more than 1% to settle near $4,156 per troy ounce, as a steady climb in global government bond yields pulled capital out of non-yielding defensive assets.

Highlights

Thursday Lock-In: Because of the Friday holiday, all final US equity index changes reflect performance up to Thursday's close.
Milestone Rebounds: The S&P 500 successfully reclaimed the critical 7,500 level, while the Dow Jones Industrial Average added to its historic posture above 51,500.
Safe Haven Deflation: The U.S. Dollar Index (DXY) weakened below 100 as the tangible thawing of Middle Eastern maritime friction reduced demand for defensive cash holdings.
Agricultural Basis Shifts: In the physical markets, domestic cash grain basis levels remained steady, though electronic ag futures experienced light, pre-holiday positioning ahead of next week's updated acreage forecasts.

Mode Performance (Wk = From Monday Open)

Equity Indices Performance

Dow Jones: 51,564.70 | D: Closed | Wk: +0.65%
S&P 500: 7,500.58 | D: Closed | Wk: +1.10%
Nasdaq: 26,517.93 | D: Closed | Wk: +1.90%
Russell 2000: 2,895.50 | D: Closed | Wk: +0.45%
Magnificent 7 (Wk = From Monday Open)

Microsoft (MSFT): $389.12 | D: Closed | Wk: +0.38%
Nvidia (NVDA): $222.10 | D: Closed | Wk: +0.64%
Alphabet (GOOGL): $331.40 | D: Closed | Wk: +0.39%
Amazon (AMZN): $222.80 | D: Closed | Wk: +0.76%
Meta (META): $601.50 | D: Closed | Wk: +0.39%
Tesla (TSLA): $395.10 | D: Closed | Wk: +0.41%

Bonds, Commodities & Volatility

10-Year Treasury Yield: 4.45% — US Bond markets closed; holding steady near weekly support.
U.S. Dollar Index (DXY): 99.42 — Sliding as easing geopolitical stress dampens greenback demand.

WTI Crude: $77.85 | D: -0.45% | Wk: -5.30%
Brent Crude: $81.00 | D: +1.02% | Wk: -6.80%
Gold Futures: $4,156.00 | D: -1.15% | Wk: -1.15%
Bitcoin (BTC): $75,680 | D: +0.70% | Wk: +0.70%
Volatility (VIX): 14.65 (Closed)

Notes and Outlook
The truncation of this week's trading layout didn't stop capital allocators from completely resetting the macro playing field. The official signing of the maritime memorandum effectively breaks the energy policy trap that has paralyzed the markets since early spring. With Brent crude falling back toward the $80 floor, structural inflation expectations are taking a massive step backward, opening up a far more accommodating environment for risk assets as we enter the back half of June.

Looking Ahead – Key Events & Levels to Watch (June 22–26)

Post-Holiday Reopening: Monday's open will serve as the first major opportunity for domestic equity desks to trade the full details of the Middle Eastern ceasefire agreements.

Switzerland Peace Progress: Energy and agricultural desks will maintain a strict watch on geopolitical channels to see if negotiators establish a firm resume date for the delayed nuclear talks.

Core PCE Print: The upcoming Personal Consumption Expenditures data release will stand as the absolute focal point for fixed-income traders looking to gauge the Federal Reserve's next policy shift.

Closing Summary
Friday, June 19, brought a quiet conclusion to an incredibly intense stretch of macro trading. While domestic exchange floors were dark for the holiday, the structural reality of a reopened Strait of Hormuz and a tech-led equity cushion means the markets are fundamentally healthier than they were a week ago.

 As institutional participants return to their desks next week, lower energy friction and a revived technology complex suggest the path of least resistance remains higher.
#StockMarket #HormuzCeasefire #JuneteenthHoliday

Axios | In an extensive interview with Marc Caputo, Donald Trump offered unfiltered assessments on Iran War : 

1/ Addressing questions of regime change, Trump distinguished between the current and former leadership: "Khamenei Jr. is different from the father. They're different people." Regarding Mojtaba Khamenei, he noted, "He’s got a certain braveness because he was badly injured."

2/ Trump offered a biting assessment of Iran’s messaging: "They've been taunting you and everybody else for 47 years. They're very good with the press. They have a great fake media."

3/ Trump highlighted his aggressive tactics to enforce compliance: "I took out their biggest bridge because they showed up late at a meeting. That’s their George Washington Bridge. I wiped it out in three minutes."

4/ He claimed his military posture was the only thing stopping an existential threat: "Had we not hit them, they would've had a nuclear weapon. They would've used it on Israel... If it weren't for Donald Trump, Israel would've been eviscerated."

5/ Dismissing calls from U.S. "hardliners" for total regime change, Trump cited the practical limits of air power: "If we're not going to put boots on the ground... they go deep into the caves. They're called granite caves. They're very powerful. They go deep, and then when we stop, they'll come up, and they'll probably be the same leaders."

6/ He warned that uncontrolled escalation would cripple the global energy market: "We would right now have the Strait of Hormuz totally closed. It would have mines all over it, and it would have missiles flying over billion-dollar ships. Those ships would never sail. We wouldn't have oil for months."

7/ Trump emphasized his personal sway over Israeli policy: "The relationship with Netanyahu... it's good, but we have to keep him a little bit sane." When asked if he could prevent an Israeli strike on Lebanon, he stated: "Yeah, I will be. They have a lot of respect for me, and they do as I say."

Look a little closer, historical facts show that every administration since 1979 has actively worked to counter, disrupt, and penalize Iranian proxy funding and militant activity, I could name something every administration has done since Reagan to confront Iranian state sponsored terrorism that had better results than Trump's.
The JCPOA successfully froze Iran’s nuclear development by subjecting it to strict international inspections. Pulling out of the deal removed oversight, allowing Iran to resume refining uranium closer to weapons grade levels, and that sanctions alone have not stopped Iran's regional operations. This are the ramifications of having a leader that makes decisions on bitterness and jealousy alone, period.
And clearing the shipping lanes, removing mines, and normalizing supply chains through the Strait of Hormuz will take months. Petroleum analysts expect a full return to baseline prices to stretch well into 2027. And oil prices ticked back up slightly in June because the administration explicitly noted the deal isn't fully finalized. The underlying consumer confidence actually edged downward in June. And long-term interest rates have remained elevated, even Trump's brand new Fed Chair said he is NOT lowering interest rates, in fact they will most likely get a hike. 
The 2015 JCPOA was a highly detailed, hundreds of pages long document packed with strict, internationally monitored technical limits on Iran's nuclear capabilities. The current MOU relies heavily on good faith and gives Iran immediate oil revenue, and leaves regional allies like Israel deeply skeptical. Even Republicans are saying it is a complete SURRENDER and failure.
And finally yes, of course we want to destroy Trump and his incompetent administration, using whatever it takes. Our focus is on removing the rampant corruption, overturning conservative judicial majorities, stopping ICE from torturing and terrorizing immigrant families, rejoining international climate pacts, funding green energy, reversing corporate tax cuts to fund social programs, protecting and expanding the Affordable Care Act, strengthening programs like Medicaid and Social Security, protecting traditional governance and peaceful transfers of power, expanding ballot access and stopping restrictive voting laws, restoring public faith in federal agencies and the FBI, and recommitting to international bodies like NATO. These are all things the majority of Americans want to see happen. So yes, see you in November!
                       📣🌊✌️🗳️🗽

3/11 🛢️ MIDDLE EAST — Iran War Day 112. Tanker traffic is resuming through the Strait of Hormuz under the US-Iran memorandum, though no outbound vessels were seen leaving the Persian Gulf on Friday morning. The main central route remains closed with an estimated 80 mines to clear. #IranWar #Hormuz #Oil #MiddleEast

Let’s look at the actual math instead of the hype. The war with Iran cost an astronomical amount per day in active military deployments, destroyed equipment, and strategic aid—pushing the total bill past $300 billion. On top of that financial drain, thousands of civilian and service member lives were cut short.

Now people are expecting gas to magically plunge overnight just because an interim deal was signed? Energy experts are explicitly warning this isn't an instant fix. The U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) notes that traffic through the Strait of Hormuz will take several months to ramp back up to pre-conflict levels, a full recovery they don’t expect until early 2027.

On top of that, Morgan Stanley's oil strategists have stated it will take weeks just for tanker flows to normalize, leaving the market in a massive supply deficit for the third quarter. The waterway is blocked with up to 80 sea mines that still need clearance, and shipping firms have already rerouted contracts around Africa for the rest of the year. Rebuilding trade routes and lowering shipping risk takes serious time. Gas isn't dropping 'like a rock' when the global supply chain is still digging itself out of a war zone. Celebrating this as an immediate 'win' is pure delusion.

@Our_Country_1st @StonKold @BeBipartisan @DavidShuster You’re ignoring the financial hit they’ve taken.

Due to the strict U.S. naval blockade targeting Iranian ports and the Strait of Hormuz, the inability to export its crude oil is costing Iran an estimated $150 million to $400 million per day in lost revenue.

The physical market gives a different read. US crude inventories are at their lowest since 1985, yet WTI calendar spreads haven't reflected that inventory tightness the way you'd expect. OPEC+ is simultaneously rolling back quota cuts while holding over 5 million barrels per day of spare capacity — so the price signal is navigating both a geopolitical risk reprice and a structural supply overhang. The divergence between physical tightness and financial price usually resolves in favor of the inventory signal, but the spare capacity buffer is what makes this cycle unusual.

The physical market gives a different read. US crude inventories are at their lowest since 1985, yet WTI calendar spreads haven't reflected that inventory tightness the way you'd expect. OPEC+ is simultaneously rolling back quota cuts while sitting on over 5 million barrels per day of spare capacity — so the price signal is navigating both a geopolitical risk reprice and a structural supply overhang. The divergence between physical tightness and financial price usually resolves in favor of the inventory signal, but the spare capacity buffer is what makes this cycle unusual.The physical market gives a different read. The divergence between physical tightness and financial price usually resolves in favor of the inventory signal, but the spare capacity buffer is what makes this cycle unusual.

This is the official story from the US Government, the EIA:

US Commercial Crude Oil Inventories were drawn down by -8.26m barrels for the week ending June 12th.  This brought US Commercial Crude inventories to 418.2m barrels.

The average draw over the last four weeks is -6.71m.

If we assume a weekly draw of 6.71m barrels for the week ending today, June 19th, that would bring commercial inventories down to 411.5m barrels.

The operational floor is said to be around 350m barrels.  Oil in pipelines can’t be drained, the pipelines require hydraulic pressure.  Sludge in the bottom of tanks, etc.

This leaves US Commercial Crude Inventories at just 61.5m barrels.  With an average draw of -6.7m barrels per week, the US has just nine weeks left.

Iran just closed the Strait again.  JD Vance has cancelled his trip to Switzerland to sign a peace agreement with Iran.  The clock is ticking.  Brent short interest is at a 5-year high.

At what point do the markets panic?  8 weeks?  7 weeks?  6 weeks?  It could be any day now.  Cushing was at the operational minimum inventory level of 20m barrels as of June 12th.

text:
US crude inventories are at their lowest since 1985, yet WTI timespreads are not signalling the tightness you might expect.   

In the week ending 12 June, US crude stocks drew by 8.3 mb, taking outright levels to their lowest since 1985. Cushing fell to levels near operational minimums, and PADD 2 stocks hit their lowest since 2014. But the latest 40 mb SPR exchange, higher Permian crude inflows to Cushing and the announcement of a US–Iran MoU are all working to ease the near-term supply picture.   

Across products, the MoU is already shifting trade economics: jet export demand from the Gulf Coast is expected to slow, while Asian LPG buyers have adopted a wait-and-see stance ahead of potential freight rate easing through Hormuz. Our latest US Oil Weekly breaks down the draw across all the barrels.   

Read more: https://t.co/byGRebrgDK 
#EnergyAspects #OOTT #OilInventories #OilStocks

The $150 billion ADNOC (Abu Dhabi National Oil Company) capex commitment is the most consequential supply-side infrastructure bet in the Gulf in decades. UAE leaving OPEC+ quota constraints while building to 5.2 mbd — all routable around Hormuz via the Fujairah bypass — structurally shifts the pricing power in every future Hormuz disruption. Every UAE barrel that doesn't need to transit the strait reduces Iran's leverage over global supply. The ADNOC build-out isn't just a national capacity story; it's a permanent change to the geopolitics of the strait.

The Permian answer to every oil price spike for the past decade was "shale will fix it." That assumption was load-bearing for every bearish supply model from the IEA down. If Permian growth is decelerating — tier-1 inventory depleted, producers returning capital rather than chasing volumes, well productivity rolling over — then the non-OPEC supply buffer shrinks exactly when Hormuz is operating at sub-70% capacity. Two structural supply arguments converging simultaneously isn't coincidence. It's the timeline for a supply shock the market has been discounting since 2022.

text:
Evening Financial Brief — June 19, 2026
𝗨𝗞 𝗯𝗼𝗿𝗿𝗼𝘄𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗵𝗼𝗼𝘁𝘀 𝘄𝗵𝗶𝗹𝗲 𝗕𝗼𝗘 𝗵𝗼𝗹𝗱𝘀; 𝗦𝗘𝗖/𝗖𝗙𝗧𝗖 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗣𝗥𝗔 𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗻 𝗱𝗲𝗿𝗶𝘃𝗮𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲𝘀 𝗿𝘂𝗹𝗲𝗯𝗼𝗼𝗸𝘀; 𝗛𝗼𝗿𝗺𝘂𝘇 𝘁𝗿𝗮𝗳𝗳𝗶𝗰 𝗿𝗶𝘀𝗲𝘀 𝗽𝗼𝘀𝘁-𝗱𝗲𝗮𝗹; 𝗢𝗣𝗘𝗖 𝗽𝘂𝘀𝗵𝗲𝘀 𝗯𝗮𝗰𝗸 𝗼𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗜𝗘𝗔.

• 𝗨𝗞 𝗠𝗮𝘆 𝗯𝗼𝗿𝗿𝗼𝘄𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘀𝘂𝗿𝗴𝗲𝘀 𝘁𝗼 £𝟮𝟯.𝟯𝗯𝗻, £𝟱.𝟲𝗯𝗻 𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿 𝗢𝗕𝗥 𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗲𝗰𝗮𝘀𝘁
ONS reports public sector net borrowing hit £23.3bn in May, up 30.4% year-on-year and £5.6bn above the OBR's March forecast, with debt interest at £11.7bn the highest May on record. BoE held Bank Rate at 3.75% the same day, citing lingering inflation pressure from the Middle East energy shock; gilt issuance and QT math now face fresh strain.
Source: ONS Public Sector Finances, UK: May 2026, BBC News

• 𝗦𝗘𝗖 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗖𝗙𝗧𝗖 𝗷𝗼𝗶𝗻𝘁 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗼𝗻 𝗧𝗶𝘁𝗹𝗲 𝗩𝗜𝗜 𝗱𝗲𝗿𝗶𝘃𝗮𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲𝘀 𝗱𝗲𝗳𝗶𝗻𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀
Chairs Atkins and Selig opened a 60-day public comment on Dodd-Frank Title VII derivatives definitions — swaps, security-based swaps, mixed swaps, and event-based products — framing it as long-overdue harmonisation. Direct read-through to CME, ICE, Nodal and prediction-market venues on which regulator owns which contract.
Source: SEC Press Release 2026-57, Markets Media

• 𝗣𝗥𝗔 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘀𝘂𝗹𝘁𝘀 𝗼𝗻 𝗺𝗮𝗿𝗸𝗲𝘁-𝗿𝗶𝘀𝗸 𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗻𝗮𝗹 𝗺𝗼𝗱𝗲𝗹𝘀, 𝗰𝗹𝗼𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗨𝗞 𝗕𝗮𝘀𝗲𝗹 𝟯.𝟭
The Bank of England's PRA opened consultation on the Internal Model Approach under FRTB, the final piece of UK Basel 3.1, confirming implementation on 1 January 2028. Changes extend P&L attribution monitoring to three years, ease transitions from the standardised approach, and target international alignment for City dealers.
Source: Bank of England / PRA news release

• 𝗛𝗼𝗿𝗺𝘂𝘇 𝘁𝗮𝗻𝗸𝗲𝗿 𝘁𝗿𝗮𝗳𝗳𝗶𝗰 𝗷𝘂𝗺𝗽𝘀 𝗽𝗼𝘀𝘁-𝗨𝗦-𝗜𝗿𝗮𝗻 𝗱𝗲𝗮𝗹
CNBC reports that per Kpler, at least 20 oil tankers crossed the Strait on Thursday — the highest single-day level since 2 June — including three Saudi and one UAE VLCC, with five Iranian supertankers switching transponders back on and departing loaded. VP Vance said Iran is "honouring their end of the commitment" under the 60-day toll-free window, though total flow remains well below prewar levels of 100+ ships per day.
Source: CNBC

• 𝗢𝗣𝗘𝗖 𝗽𝘂𝘀𝗵𝗲𝘀 𝗯𝗮𝗰𝗸 𝗼𝗻 𝗜𝗘𝗔'𝘀 𝟮𝟬𝟮𝟳 𝟴𝗺𝗯/𝗱 𝘀𝘂𝗽𝗽𝗹𝘆 𝗴𝗹𝘂𝘁 𝗰𝗮𝗹𝗹
OPEC Secretary-General al-Ghais told CNBC the IEA's June Oil Market Report — projecting 2027 demand up 2mb/d against supply up 8mb/d to 110mb/d — is "not based on actual facts and figures" and that such headlines "only create more volatility." Sets up a direct OPEC vs IEA clash into next week's likely JMMC, with implications for quotas and Brent/WTI term structure.
Source: IEA Oil Market Report - June 2026, CNBC interview with OPEC Secretary-General al-Ghais
